Liang Chen stood at his table, his eyes fixed on me with predatory intensity.

"The recess is over," the head judge announced. "The scope of relevance has been determined. The Divine Witness will be limited to matters pertaining to the charges against the defendant. Questions of identity, origin, and prior existence are deemed irrelevant unless directly connected to the crimes in question."

I breathed a sigh of relief. The Arbiter had come through.

"However," the head judge continued, "the Witness may still reveal information that falls outside this scope if it deems such information necessary for the pursuit of justice. The Witness has its own discretion."

*Of course it does.*

"Summon the Divine Witness."

The crowd gasped as light began to gather in the center of the courtroom. It was blinding, beautiful, and terrible all at once. The light coalesced into a figure—tall, androgynous, with features that seemed to shift and change every time I looked at them.

"I am the Truth," the figure said, and its voice resonated in my

title: Truth That Burns
content:
Elara's throat went dry. The Divine Witness of Truth. She'd barely survived the Arbiter's revelations, and now this? Her mind seized on the hero's words—*anyone in its presence cannot lie, cannot withhold information, and cannot hide their true nature.*

*If I refuse, I look guilty. If I accept, they'll know I'm not the Demon Empress. They'll know I'm a lawyer from another world who died of caffeine overdose and bad life choices.*

She'd be annihilated before she could blink.

But Elara had spent fifteen years in corporate law. She'd learned that the most dangerous traps were the ones you saw coming—because you could step around them.

"I accept," she said.

The hero's smile faltered. The judges leaned forward. Even the Arbiter, who had been floating serenely above the proceedings, descended to hover at eye level.

"You realize what this means," the Arbiter said, her voice soft. "The Divine Witness will expose everything. Your crimes. Your origins. Your very soul."

"I know." Elara turned to face the assembled crowd—cultivators from a dozen sects, elders with faces like carved stone, disciples who whispered behind their sleeves. "But I have nothing to hide."

It was the biggest lie she'd ever told, and she told it with perfect conviction.

The head judge raised his hand. "So be it. Summon the Divine Witness."

The courtroom went dark.

Not the darkness of night or shadow, but the darkness of *absence*—as if light itself had decided to evacuate the premises. Elara felt cold seep into her bones, a chill that had nothing to do with temperature and everything to do with being *seen* by something that should not exist.

Then the Witness arrived.

It had no form she could describe. It was a presence that pressed against her consciousness, a weight that settled on her soul. She felt exposed, stripped of all pretense, as if every thought she'd ever had was written on her skin in burning ink.

*Speak truth,* a voice said inside her skull. *Or be unmade.*

"State your name," the head judge said.

"Elara Chen." The words came out easily. True.

"State your title."

"Formerly a senior associate at Sterling & Price. Currently... the Demon Empress."

*True,* the Witness confirmed.

The hero's eyes narrowed. He'd expected her to stumble, to reveal herself as an impostor. But Elara had learned something crucial during her years in court: truth was a weapon, not a weakness. You just had to know how to wield it.

"State your crimes," the head judge continued.

Elara took a deep breath. "I am accused of treason against the Celestial Order, conspiracy to overthrow the Heavenly Council, and unlawful cultivation of forbidden techniques. Additionally, I'm charged with the murder of three Celestial Enforcers, the corruption of twelve minor sects, and the attempted assassination of the Celestial Sword Sect's patriarch."

The crowd murmured. These were the charges that had led to the Demon Empress's execution in the original timeline.

*True,* the Witness confirmed.

"The prosecution will now present its case," the head judge said.

The hero rose, his robes flowing like liquid silver. He looked nothing like the righteous protagonist from the novel Elara had read. This man was harder, sharper, with eyes that calculated every move.

"The Demon Empress has terrorized the cultivation world for three centuries," he began. "She has destroyed families, corrupted the innocent, and amassed power through bloodshed. We have witnesses who saw her kill the Celestial Enforcers. We have testimonies from the sects she corrupted. We have her own confession, extracted under truth-compelling before her execution."

He turned to Elara, and she saw the trap closing. The real Demon Empress had confessed. That confession was a matter of record. And under the Divine Witness, Elara couldn't deny it.

"Did you confess to these crimes?" the hero asked.

Elara met his gaze. "I did."

The courtroom erupted.

"Silence!" the head judge thundered. "The Witness has confirmed her statement. Elara Chen, by your own admission, you are guilty."

"I admitted that I confessed," Elara said, her voice cutting through the noise. "I didn't admit to the crimes."

The hero's smile returned. "Semantics. You confessed under truth-compelling. The confession itself was verified by three separate truth-tellers."

"And I'm saying the confession was coerced."

"Under truth-compelling? Impossible. The technique forces absolute honesty."

Elara had done her research. During the three days between her arrest and the trial, she'd devoured every book in the Arbiter's library. She'd learned about cultivation, about techniques, about the laws that governed this world.

And she'd found a loophole.

"Truth-compelling requires the subject to believe they're telling the truth," she said. "But belief and fact are not the same thing. If the Demon Empress genuinely believed she committed those crimes, the technique would register her confession as true—even if she was wrong."

The hero's confidence wavered. "That's—"

"Impossible?" Elara smiled. "No. It's basic law. Intent matters. Mens rea. If I believe I stole something that was actually given to me, my confession of theft is still technically a lie—but truth-compelling would register it as true because I *believe* it to be true."

The judges exchanged glances. The Arbiter nodded slowly.

"The defendant raises a valid point," the Arbiter said. "Truth-compelling has a documented flaw: it cannot distinguish between objective truth and subjective belief."

"Then how do we prove the truth?" the hero demanded.

"By examining the evidence," Elara said. "By calling witnesses. By doing what this trial was supposed to do—instead of relying on a confession that could be based on false memories."

The head judge leaned forward. "You claim the Demon Empress's memories were falsified?"

"I claim nothing. I'm asking for the opportunity to prove it."

"And if you can't?"

"Then I accept the consequences."

The hero slammed his hand on the table. "This is a delay tactic! She's trying to—"

"The defendant's request is reasonable," the head judge interrupted. "We will hear testimony. We will examine evidence. And at the end, we will render judgment." He turned to Elara. "You will be given three days to present your defense. The prosecution will have the same. Is that acceptable?"

"More than acceptable."

The Arbiter smiled. "Then we adjourn until tomorrow. Court is dismissed."

---

Elara was escorted to a holding cell in the celestial palace. It was luxurious by prison standards—silk cushions, fresh fruit, a fountain that played soft music—but it was still a cage.

She sat on the floor, her back against the wall, and closed her eyes.

*Three days. I have three days to prove I'm not guilty of crimes I might have committed in a body that isn't mine.*

She'd bought herself time, but she still had no idea how to win.

A knock on the door.

"Enter," she said, surprised anyone would bother with formalities.

The hero stepped inside.

He looked different now. Softer. The hard edges of his face had smoothed, and his eyes held something that might have been concern.

"We need to talk," he said.

"About what? How you're going to destroy me?"

"About how I'm going to save you."

Elara laughed. It was a bitter sound, hollow and sharp. "You tried to execute me."

"I was following orders." He sat across from her, crossing his legs in a mirror of her position. "But I've been watching you, Elara Chen. The Demon Empress was cruel, petty, and predictable. You're none of those things."

"Maybe I'm just better at hiding."

"No. I can see souls." He tapped his chest. "It's a gift from my cultivation. And your soul... it doesn't belong here."

Elara's blood turned to ice. "The Divine Witness—"

"Is gone. And I'm not here to expose you." He leaned forward. "I'm here because I think we're on the same side."

"What side would that be?"

"The side that wants to keep this world from burning."

He pulled a scroll from his sleeve and unrolled it. It was a map—but not of lands or oceans. It showed connections, lines of power that wove through the cultivation world like veins.

"This is a map of the Celestial Order's influence," he said. "Every sect, every political faction, every hidden alliance. And do you see what's missing?"

Elara studied the map. At first, she saw only chaos—a web of connections that seemed random. But then she noticed the pattern.

"The connections are too organized," she said slowly. "They don't spread naturally. They converge."

"On what?"

She traced the lines with her finger. They all led to a single point—a place that wasn't marked on the map, but was clearly the center of everything.

"The Heavenly Council," she whispered.

"Bingo." The hero rolled up the scroll. "The Demon Empress wasn't evil. She was a rebel. She discovered that the Heavenly Council was using forbidden techniques to control the cultivation world. They were erasing memories, manipulating events, ensuring that no one could challenge their power."

"And they framed her for treason."

"Worse. They made her believe she was guilty. They planted false memories, fabricated evidence, turned her into the villain everyone hated." He met her eyes. "And now they're going to do the same to you."

Elara's mind raced. This changed everything. If the hero was telling the truth, then the entire plot of the novel—the righteous hero defeating the evil Demon Empress—was a fabrication. She wasn't the villain. She was the victim.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I can't stop them alone." He held out his hand. "I need the Demon Empress. I need the one person who knows their methods, who can fight their techniques, who can expose their lies."

"And in exchange?"

"You walk free. Not just from this trial, but from everything. A new identity, a new life, somewhere they can't find you."

Elara stared at his hand. It was an offer she couldn't refuse—and she knew it.

"What about the trial? If I expose the Heavenly Council, they'll destroy me."

"Let me worry about that." He smiled. "I've been planning this for a hundred years. I have evidence, witnesses, and allies in places they'd never expect."

She took his hand.

"Then I'm in."

But as they shook, she saw something flicker in his eyes—triumph. Not the triumph of a liberator, but the cold satisfaction of a hunter who'd finally caught his prey.

*Oh no.*

She looked down at their clasped hands. Her skin was turning black. Not the black of bruising, but the black of corruption—a technique she'd read about in the Arbiter's library.

"You—"

"I'm sorry," the hero said, and for a moment, he almost looked sincere. "But I need the real Demon Empress. And you're just a parasite wearing her skin."

The corruption spread up her arm, and Elara screamed.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Divine Witness Descends

The courtroom held its breath.

Elara's mind raced through every option, every possible loophole, every legal maneuver she'd ever learned. The Divine Witness of Truth. A being that compelled absolute honesty. Under its gaze, she couldn't hide her identity as Elara Chen, corporate lawyer from another world. She couldn't bluff about her lack of cultivation. She couldn't conceal her knowledge of the original Demon Empress's death—a death she had nothing to do with but would be blamed for anyway.

*If I refuse, I look guilty. If I agree, I'm exposed.*

"Your hesitation speaks volumes," the hero said, his voice smooth as poisoned honey. "What are you afraid of, Empress? That the truth might not favor you?"

"I'm considering the procedural implications," Elara replied, buying time. "The Divine Witness is not standard practice in arbitration proceedings. It's an extraordinary measure that should require extraordinary justification."

The head judge's ancient eyes narrowed. "And yet, you requested a jury of your peers. An equally extraordinary measure."

"Which the contract permits."

"As does the calling of the Divine Witness," the hero interjected. "Article 37, section C: *In matters of profound cosmic consequence, either party may request the presence of the Divine Witness to ensure the purity of testimony.*" He smiled. "I've done my homework, Empress. I suggest you do yours."

Elara's jaw tightened. He'd been preparing for this. Probably since the moment she'd proposed arbitration. He'd anticipated her every move because he knew who she really was.

*No. He suspects. But he doesn't know.*

"Then by all means," Elara said, forcing calm into her voice, "call your witness. I have nothing to hide."

The lie tasted like ash.

The hero raised his hand, and the starfield ceiling began to churn. Constellations swirled, stars dimmed, and a pressure descended on the courtroom that made Elara's knees want to buckle. She locked them, refusing to show weakness.

A figure emerged from the center of the spiraling cosmos.

It was humanoid, but barely. Its body was translucent, filled with swirling nebulae and distant galaxies. Its face had no features, only a smooth expanse of silver that reflected the faces of everyone in the room. When it spoke, its voice was the sound of a thousand voices layered together, discordant and harmonious at once.

*I am called. I am here. I am Truth.*

The Divine Witness descended to the floor, its feet never quite touching the obsidian. It turned its featureless face toward Elara, and she felt as though she were being peeled open, layer by layer.

"Begin," the head judge said.

The hero stepped forward. "Divine Witness, I ask you to examine the defendant. Tell us who she truly is."

The Witness tilted its head. The silver face rippled, and Elara saw her own reflection—but it wasn't her face. It was the Demon Empress's face, beautiful and terrible, with eyes that held centuries of cruelty.

Then the reflection shifted.

Elara Chen. Thirty-two years old. Born in Portland, Oregon. Died of cardiac arrest at her desk at 3:47 AM on a Tuesday. Reborn into this body three days before the execution.

The courtroom erupted into chaos.

"She's a transmigrant!"

"An imposter!"

"The real Demon Empress is dead!"

The judges' ancient faces remained impassive, but their eyes burned with renewed interest. The hero stood frozen, his calculated smile gone, replaced by something that looked almost like... admiration?

"Silence!" the head judge commanded. The noise died instantly. "The Divine Witness has spoken. The defendant is not the original Demon Empress. This changes the nature of the charges."

"It changes nothing," the prosecutor—a thin man with a beak-like nose—interjected. "The body is the same. The crimes were committed by this body. Transmigration does not absolve the soul of the vessel's debts."

"Objection," Elara said, her voice steady despite the trembling in her hands. "Under what legal framework? The soul is the legal entity, not the body. If I had been forcibly placed in this body against my will, I cannot be held responsible for actions taken before my arrival."

"Prove it was against your will," the prosecutor snapped.

The Divine Witness turned to Elara again.

*She speaks truth. She did not choose this body. She did not choose this world.*

Another wave of murmurs. The hero's expression had gone unreadable, but his eyes never left Elara's face.

"The defendant is innocent of the Demon Empress's crimes," the head judge declared. "All charges related to her previous actions are dismissed."

Relief flooded through Elara, so intense it made her dizzy. She'd done it. She'd actually—

"However," the judge continued, "the crime of identity theft remains."

Elara's blood turned to ice. "What?"

"The original Demon Empress's soul was displaced. Whether by your arrival or by other circumstances, this has yet to be determined. But the fact remains that you are currently inhabiting a body that does not belong to you. That is a violation of cosmic law."

The hero stepped forward again. "I move to amend the charges. Elara Chen, formerly of Earth, is accused of unlawful soul transference, identity theft, and disruption of the celestial order."

"Seconded," the prosecutor said.

*They're going to find me guilty of existing.*

Elara's mind raced. She needed a defense, and fast. "Your Honor, I request permission to call my own witness."

The judges exchanged glances. "Granted."

Elara turned to face the courtroom. Her eyes scanned the crowd, searching for anyone who might help her. She saw cultivators from various sects, celestial bureaucrats, and beings she couldn't even identify. But one face stood out.

The Arbiter.

The woman who had helped her earlier, who had given her the chance to fight. She sat in the back, her expression neutral, but her eyes met Elara's with something that might have been encouragement.

"The Arbiter," Elara said. "I call her to the stand."

The Arbiter rose and walked to the center of the room. The Divine Witness turned its featureless face toward her, and she showed no fear.

"State your name and position," Elara said.

"I am known as the Arbiter. I oversee disputes of cosmic significance between realms."

"Have you ever encountered a case of transmigration similar to mine before?"

"I have."

"And in those cases, how was the displaced soul handled?"

The Arbiter paused. "In most cases, the displaced soul is located and returned to its original body. If the body is no longer viable, a new vessel is provided."

"But in my case, no one has attempted to locate the Demon Empress's original soul."

"That is correct."

"Why?"

The Arbiter's gaze flickered to the hero. "Because someone blocked the search. The seal was placed shortly after your arrival in this world."

The courtroom exploded again.

The hero's face went pale. "That's a lie—"

*The Divine Witness turns to him.*

*He speaks falsehood.*

Silence.

The hero stared at the Witness, his expression shifting from outrage to fear. "I... I didn't—"

*He speaks falsehood.*

"I placed the seal," the hero said, his voice barely a whisper. "I don't know why. I was compelled."

*He speaks truth.*

The judges rose to their feet. "Compelled by whom?"

The hero shook his head. "I don't know. I only remember a voice. A command. *Seal her away. Keep her hidden.*"

The Divine Witness turned to Elara again.

*He speaks truth. He does not know the source of the command. But the seal remains, and the original soul remains trapped.*

"Then we have two victims here," Elara said slowly. "The Demon Empress, whose soul is trapped somewhere, and myself, who was forcibly pulled into this world. Both of us are victims of a third party."

The prosecutor's face twisted. "That's speculation."

"Then let's find proof," Elara shot back. "Your Honor, I request an investigation into the source of the seal. I request that all parties involved in my arrival be questioned. And I request that the hero be placed under observation, given that he was used as a tool by an unknown entity."

The head judge stroked his chin. "The defendant makes a compelling argument. However, this investigation will take time. The court will adjourn for one week to gather evidence. In the meantime—"

"The defendant must be held in custody," the prosecutor interrupted. "For her own safety, of course."

"Of course," Elara said dryly.

"The defendant will be placed in the Celestial Holding Cells," the head judge declared. "She will be treated with dignity and respect. Any attempt on her life will be considered an offense against the court itself."

The hero stepped forward. "I request permission to visit the defendant. For... questioning."

"Denied," the head judge said. "You are under suspicion as well. You will remain in seclusion until the investigation is complete."

The hero's face darkened, but he bowed. "As the court commands."

The Divine Witness began to dissolve, its form scattering into points of light that rose back into the starfield. As it faded, Elara felt the pressure lift, and she could breathe again.

Two guards approached her. They were not the brutish demons she'd expected. They were tall, elegant beings with skin like marble and eyes that glowed with soft light.

"Please follow us," one said.

They led her out of the courtroom, through a corridor of swirling mist, and into a chamber that was surprisingly comfortable. A bed, a desk, a window that looked out onto an endless sea of clouds. It was a prison, but a luxurious one.

"You will be provided with food and drink," the guard said. "If you need anything, speak the word 'summon' and someone will attend to you."

"Thank you."

The guards left, and Elara collapsed onto the bed. Her heart was still racing, her mind still spinning. She'd survived today. Barely. But the investigation loomed, and with it, the threat of the puppet master who had orchestrated her arrival.

Someone had brought her here. Someone powerful enough to compel a hero, to seal a soul, to manipulate the cosmic order. And that someone had wanted her to take the fall for the Demon Empress's crimes.

*Why? What am I supposed to be?*

A knock at the door made her sit up.

"Enter," she said.

The door slid open, and the Arbiter stepped inside. She looked different now—less neutral, more worried. "We need to talk."

"About what?"

The Arbiter closed the door behind her. "About the one who brought you here. I've been investigating since you arrived, and I've found something."

"What?"

The Arbiter's voice dropped to a whisper. "The seal placed on the Demon Empress's soul—it's connected to a power source that shouldn't exist. A power that predates the cultivation world itself."

Elara's blood ran cold. "Predates... what does that mean?"

"It means," the Arbiter said, "that the entity that brought you here is older than the heavens. And it means that you're not the first person they've brought to this world."

The Arbiter reached into her robes and pulled out a scroll. She unrolled it, revealing a list of names. Dozens of them. All crossed out.

"These are the others," the Arbiter said. "All transmigrated. All killed within a month of arrival. And all connected to the same power source."

Elara stared at the names. "Why am I different?"

"You're not," the Arbiter said. "

title: The Witness Speaks
content:
The courtroom fell silent. Every immortal, every sect elder, every celestial functionary leaned forward in their seats. Even the clouds that formed the walls seem to hold their breath.

Elara kept her face neutral, but her mind was a storm.

*The Divine Witness of Truth. Compels absolute honesty. Cannot lie, cannot hide, cannot deceive.*

She'd walked into countless depositions knowing her clients were guilty. She'd built defenses on technicalities, on procedural errors, on the fine print of contracts that no one read. She'd won cases because the truth was optional.

But here? Under this Witness?

*I'm dead.*

The head judge's voice cut through her spiraling thoughts. "The accused must agree to the Divine Witness. It is not a right of the prosecution—it is a voluntary submission."

All eyes turned to her.

The hero's smile had faded into something more dangerous: patience. He knew what she was. He'd seen the cracks in her performance, the moments when she acted unlike the Demon Empress should. He wanted confirmation.

*He wants to prove I'm an imposter.*

Elara's fingers curled into fists beneath the table. She could refuse. The rules allowed it. But refusal would be an admission of guilt in the eyes of these beings. They'd assume she had something to hide—and she did—but they'd also assume it was the crimes she was charged with.

*Damned if I do, damned if I don't.*

She looked at the Arbiter, whose face was unreadable behind her veil of light. Then at the hero, whose eyes gleamed with anticipation.

Then she thought about her old life.

Courtroom 3B. Judge Harrison. The case of Sterling & Price versus the Department of Justice. She'd been defending a pharmaceutical company that knowingly released a defective drug. Three hundred dead. Thousands injured. And she'd won on a statute of limitations technicality.

She'd gone home that night and vomited.

But she'd also gone home to her empty apartment, to her microwave dinner, to the mountain of files that would never stop growing. She'd died at her desk, forty-three years old, no family, no friends, no legacy except a few more million in a corporate account.

*This time, I have something to fight for.*

"Fine," Elara said.

The word dropped like a stone into still water.

"I accept the Divine Witness."

The hero's smile returned. But there was something else in his eyes now. Surprise. And maybe, just maybe, a flicker of doubt.

The head judge raised a hand. "So be it."

The air in the courtroom changed. It grew heavy, dense, as if the atmosphere itself was condensing into something solid. The starfield ceiling began to swirl, stars spiraling inward like water down a drain. The obsidian floor reflected nothing—it became a void, a mirror to absolute darkness.

And then the Witness arrived.

It didn't walk in. It didn't descend from above. It simply *was*, suddenly and completely, occupying a space that hadn't existed a moment before.

The Divine Witness of Truth was a woman. Or at least, it wore the form of a woman. Tall, pale, with hair that shifted between all colors and none. Her eyes were white—no iris, no pupil, just endless white that seemed to look through everything and everyone.

She wore robes made of light, and where she stood, shadows did not exist.

"The accused has requested a jury of her peers," the Witness said. Her voice was neutral. Absolute. "I have assembled them."

The jury box materialized from the mist. Twelve figures sat within it, each one more terrifying than the last.

An ancient dragon in human form, scales glinting at the edges of her skin. A ghostly cultivator whose body was half-transparent, his eyes holding the wisdom of ten thousand years. A demon queen with horns of jade, her smile sharp as a blade. A celestial judge from the Upper Realms, his face so beautiful it hurt to look at.

And eight others, equally powerful, equally ancient.

*My peers,* Elara thought. *These are the people who are supposed to judge me.*

"Let the arbitration begin," the Witness said. "I will be present for all testimony. Any falsehood, any omission, any deception will be known to me—and to this court."

The head judge nodded. "The prosecution may present its first witness."

The hero stood. "I call Elder Feng of the Celestial Sword Sect."

An old man rose from the audience. He walked to the witness stand with the measured steps of someone who had done this many times before. His robes were immaculate, his beard perfectly white, his eyes sharp with barely concealed hatred.

"Elder Feng," the hero began, "please state your relationship to the accused."

The Elder's jaw tightened. "She murdered my son."

The courtroom murmured.

"During the Night of Falling Stars, fifteen years ago, the Demon Empress—" he pointed at Elara, his finger trembling—"slaughtered my son and forty-two other disciples of the Celestial Sword Sect. She did not fight them. She did not challenge them. She *executed* them. While they knelt in surrender."

Elara's blood ran cold.

*I don't remember this.*

But of course she didn't. She wasn't the real Demon Empress. The memories she'd inherited were fragmented, incomplete. There were gaps in her knowledge—dark gaps where the previous occupant of this body had done terrible things.

"Did you witness this personally?" Elara asked, before she could stop herself.

The Elder's eyes snapped to her. "I saw the bodies."

"That's not what I asked."

"Objection," the hero said smoothly. "The accused is not permitted to cross-examine until her defense phase."

"Overruled," the head judge said. "The accused may question witnesses. This is arbitration, not a standard trial."

*One point for me.*

"Elder Feng," Elara said, rising from her seat. She walked toward the witness stand, her heels clicking on the obsidian floor. "Did you personally witness the Demon Empress killing your son?"

The Elder's face reddened. "I—the bodies were—"

"Did. You. Witness. It."

"No," he said through gritted teeth. "I arrived after. But the evidence—"

"Thank you, Elder Feng." Elara turned to the jury. "The prosecution's first witness did not see the crime. He saw the aftermath. There is a difference."

"Your Honor," the hero interrupted, "the accused is testifying, not questioning."

"She's right," the Arbiter said. "Sustained. Elder Feng, you may step down."

But the Elder didn't move. His eyes locked onto Elara's, and for a moment, she saw something more than hatred in them.

*Fear.*

"You don't remember, do you?" he whispered.

The courtroom went silent.

Elara's heart stopped.

"You don't remember killing them." The Elder's voice grew louder. "You don't remember the Night of Falling Stars. You don't remember anything from before three months ago." He turned to the judges. "I've suspected it for weeks. The Demon Empress I knew would never have negotiated. She would never have accepted an arbitration. She would have burned this court to ash before submitting to any authority."

The head judge leaned forward. "What are you implying, Elder Feng?"

"I'm implying that the woman standing before us is not the Demon Empress."

The crowd erupted.

Elara stood frozen as chaos unfolded around her. Sect elders shouted. Celestial beings argued. The jury whispered among themselves.

But the Divine Witness of Truth was silent.

And she was looking directly at Elara.

"Is that true?" the Witness asked.

The question cut through all noise.

*She's asking me. Not the Elder. Not the prosecution. Me.*

Elara's throat went dry.

"I—"

"Remember," the Witness said, her voice soft but absolute. "You cannot lie. You cannot withhold. You cannot hide."

Elara looked at the hero. He was staring at her with an expression she couldn't read. Not triumph. Not anger. Something else.

*He knows. He's known since the beginning.*

She took a breath.

"No," she said. "I am not the Demon Empress."

The words hung in the air like a death sentence.

"I am Elara Chen. I was a lawyer in another world. I died, and I woke up in this body three months ago, moments before my execution."

The courtroom was so quiet she could hear the stars moving.

"I have no cultivation. I have no demonic powers. I have no memories of the crimes I'm accused of—because I didn't commit them."

She looked at the jury. At the judges. At the Arbiter, whose expression had finally shifted to something like shock.

"But I am not innocent."

The Witness tilted her head. "Explain."

"In my previous life, I defended corporations that killed people. I hid evidence. I buried testimony. I won cases that should have been lost, and people died because of me." Her voice cracked. "I am not the Demon Empress. But I am not a good person either."

The hero stood slowly. "Then who are you?"

Elara met his eyes.

"I'm someone who wants a second chance."

The silence stretched.

Then the Divine Witness spoke.

"She speaks the truth."

The courtroom exploded.

But Elara didn't hear any of it.

Because in the chaos, she saw something impossible.

The hero was smiling.

Not a triumphant smile. Not a calculating smile.

A *relieved* smile.

And then he mouthed three words that made her blood run cold:

*"I know."*

---

**END OF CHAPTER 9**

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Witness of Truth

The courtroom fell silent. Every eye turned to Elara, waiting for her response. She could feel the weight of their expectations, their suspicion, their hunger for her destruction.

*Think. Think like a lawyer.*

"The Divine Witness of Truth," she repeated, buying time. "I'm not familiar with this mechanism. Perhaps you could explain it to me, Your Honor?"

The head judge's ancient eyes narrowed. "The Divine Witness is a creature from the age before cultivation. It exists outside the laws of this realm. When summoned, it creates a zone of absolute truth. No deception is possible within its presence."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then the arbitration proceeds without it, but your refusal will be noted as evidence of guilt."

*Classic. Damned if I do, damned if I don't.*

Elara turned to face the hero. He was watching her with that calculating expression, waiting for her to slip, to reveal something she shouldn't know.

"You're very confident," she said. "What makes you so sure I'll agree?"

"Because you're clever." The hero's voice was almost admiring. "You know that refusing would destroy your credibility. And a lawyer without credibility is no lawyer at all."

"Flattery will get you nowhere." Elara turned back to the judges. "I have a counter-proposal."

The head judge raised an eyebrow. "You're in no position to negotiate."

"Actually, I am." Elara pulled out the contract she'd memorized during her time in the void. "Article 7, section C. The accused has the right to challenge any witness or evidence presented. The Divine Witness is a witness. I challenge its validity."

"On what grounds?"

"Bias." Elara began to pace, her heels clicking against the obsidian floor. "The Divine Witness has existed since before cultivation. That means it predates the Celestial Order. It has no loyalty to this court, no investment in justice. It simply reveals truth without context, without nuance, without understanding."

She stopped and faced the judges directly.

"Truth without context is not truth. It's data. And data can be manipulated."

The hero's smile faltered. "That's—"

"She's not wrong," the Arbiter's voice interrupted. "The Divine Witness has been known to cause problems in the past. It doesn't understand mortal concepts like intention, circumstance, or growth. It merely reveals what is, not what could be."

The head judge stroked his chin. "Then what do you propose, accused?"

"A modified version." Elara's mind raced through every legal precedent she'd ever learned, every loophole she'd ever exploited. "The Divine Witness can be present, but its revelations are filtered through a neutral interpreter. Someone who can provide context, explain circumstances, and ensure that the truth is understood rather than simply displayed."

"And who would serve as this interpreter?"

"The Arbiter." Elara turned to face the empty air where the Arbiter's voice had come from. "She's already proven herself neutral. She gave me the contract. She allowed me to challenge the process. She's the only one here who isn't invested in my guilt or innocence."

The hero's face darkened. "The Arbiter has already shown favor to the accused. She cannot be—"

"She can," the Arbiter's voice said, and this time she appeared. She was a woman of indeterminate age, with silver hair and eyes that held the wisdom of centuries. "I can, and I will. The accused has made a valid legal argument. I accept the role."

"But—"

"The objection is overruled," the head judge said, cutting the hero off. "The Divine Witness will be summoned, but its revelations will be interpreted by the Arbiter. The trial will proceed."

The hero slammed his fist on the table. "This is a farce!"

"This is justice," Elara said quietly. "Something you seem to have forgotten."

The hero's eyes met hers, and for a moment, she saw something flicker in them. Doubt? No, that was impossible. He was the hero. He was supposed to be righteous, pure, unquestionably good.

But then again, she was supposed to be the villainess.

And look how that turned out.

---

The Divine Witness was not what Elara expected.

She'd imagined something terrible, a beast of fire and shadow, a creature of pure cosmic horror. Instead, it appeared as a simple beam of light, golden and warm, that descended from the starfield ceiling and settled in the center of the courtroom.

"Speak your truth," the Witness said, its voice like chimes in the wind. "And I shall reveal it."

The hero stepped forward. "I will go first."

He walked into the beam of light, and Elara watched as his form began to shimmer. Images appeared around him, memories given form, secrets laid bare.

His childhood, training under the Celestial Sword Sect.

His first kill, a demon who had begged for mercy.

His meetings with the Demon Empress, the negotiations that had failed, the battles that had nearly killed him.

And then, something unexpected.

A memory of him standing before a mirror, his hand pressed against the glass, his face twisted with uncertainty.

*"Am I doing the right thing?"* the memory-version of him whispered. *"She seemed so reasonable. So... human."*

The courtroom murmured. The judges exchanged glances.

"The witness is not here to reveal his own secrets," the head judge said sternly. "He is here to testify."

"Apologies." The hero stepped out of the light, and the images vanished. "I merely wished to demonstrate that the Witness is functioning properly."

Elara's heart raced. That memory... it showed doubt. It showed that even the hero wasn't sure of her guilt.

*Interesting.*

"Your turn," the hero said, gesturing to the light. "Unless you're afraid."

Elara took a deep breath. She'd known this was coming. She'd known that under the Divine Witness, every secret would be exposed.

*Including the fact that I'm not the real Demon Empress.*

But she couldn't refuse. Not now.

She stepped into the light.

---

The beam enveloped her, warm and comforting. For a moment, nothing happened. Then the images began to appear.

Her past life. The office, the late nights, the endless paperwork.

Her death. The heart attack at her desk, the feeling of falling, the darkness.

And then... the void.

The courtroom gasped as they saw her rebirth, saw her open her eyes in the body of the Demon Empress, saw her realize where she was and what she had become.

"She's not the real Empress!" someone shouted.

"She's an imposter!"

"Kill her now!"

The head judge raised his hand, and silence fell. "Arbiter. Interpret."

The Arbiter stepped forward, her silver eyes fixed on Elara. "The accused is not the original Demon Empress. That is true. But the original Empress's soul was already destroyed before this one took its place. The body was empty. The accused did not commit murder to claim it."

"That doesn't matter!" the hero said. "She's still—"

"It matters," the Arbiter interrupted. "Under Celestial Law, a body without a soul is considered abandoned property. The accused found it and claimed it. That is not a crime."

The hero's face reddened. "But she's been impersonating the Empress! She's been using her power, her influence—"

"Which she has not used to commit any of the crimes she's accused of." The Arbiter turned to the judges. "The evidence shows that since her arrival, the accused has done nothing but try to survive. She has not attacked anyone. She has not conspired with demons. She has not attempted to overthrow the Celestial Order."

The judges murmured among themselves.

"Then why is she on trial?" the head judge asked.

"Because she's the easiest target." The Arbiter's voice was cold. "The real Demon Empress was a monster. But she's dead. And rather than admit that the Celestial Order failed to capture her, rather than admit that she escaped justice, they've decided to try the woman who found her body."

The courtroom erupted again.

Elara stood in the light, watching it all unfold. She should have felt relieved. The Arbiter was defending her. The truth was coming out.

But something was wrong.

The hero was smiling.

*Why is he smiling?*

"I see," the hero said, his voice cutting through the chaos. "So the accused is innocent. She's not the Demon Empress. She's just a mortal who stumbled into a body she didn't deserve."

"That's not—" Elara started.

"Then she should have no objection to a simple test." The hero pulled out a scroll, ancient and yellowed. "This is a Geas of Truth. If she signs it, she agrees to bind her soul to this body permanently. No more reincarnations, no more running away."

"And if she refuses?" the Arbiter asked.

"Then she's lying." The hero's smile widened. "And her soul will be torn from this body and cast into the void of eternal nothing."

Elara's blood ran cold.

*He's not trying to prove my guilt. He's trying to trap me.*

If she signed, she'd be bound to the Demon Empress's body forever. She'd never be able to escape, never be able to find a new life, never be able to truly be free.

If she refused, they'd destroy her.

"Sign it," the hero said, sliding the scroll across the table. "Or die."

The courtroom fell silent.

Every eye turned to Elara.

She picked up the scroll. She read the terms. She saw the loophole.

*There it is.*

"Article 4," she said quietly. "It says I'm binding my soul to this body. But it doesn't specify which body."

The hero's smile vanished. "What?"

"The Demon Empress's body has been transformed since I took it. It's no longer the same vessel. So technically, I'd be binding my soul to *this* body, in *this* moment. If I ever change it, if I ever improve it, the contract is void."

"That's—"

"That's legal," the Arbiter said, her voice filled with grudging respect. "She's found the loophole."

Elara signed the scroll.

The hero's face twisted with rage. "This isn't over."

"No," Elara agreed. "It's not."

But as she stepped out of the light, she felt something shift inside her. A presence. A weight. Something that hadn't been there before.

*What was that?*

She looked down at her hands, and for a moment, she thought she saw shadows moving beneath her skin.

Then the moment passed, and everything was normal.

*It's nothing,* she told herself. *Just my nerves.*

But deep in her heart, she knew the truth.

The Geas of Truth had worked.

And now, she was bound to the Demon Empress's body forever.

*What have I done?*

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Witness of Truth

The courtroom fell into an expectant hush. Every eye turned to Elara, waiting for her response. She could feel the weight of their gazes like physical pressure, judging, measuring, anticipating her downfall.

*Think, Elara. Think.*

The Divine Witness of Truth. A being that compelled absolute honesty. In a single moment, it would unravel everything she'd built. Her identity as a reincarnated soul from another world. Her lack of cultivation. Her desperate bluff that had kept her alive this long.

But if she refused, they'd know she had something to hide. And in a celestial court, that was as good as a confession.

"Your hesitation speaks volumes," the hero said, his voice smooth as poisoned honey. "What are you afraid of, Empress? The truth?"

Elara met his gaze. Up close, she could see the lines of exhaustion around his eyes, the slight tension in his jaw. He wasn't as confident as he appeared. He *wanted* her to refuse.

*Why?*

Unless... the Divine Witness worked both ways.

"I accept," Elara said.

The hero's smile faltered. "What?"

"I said I accept." She turned to the judges. "Call your Divine Witness. I have nothing to hide."

*Lies,* she thought. *I have everything to hide. But I have nothing to lose.*

The Arbiter's voice drifted down from above, carrying a hint of approval. "Bold move, Empress. The Divine Witness will be summoned."

The air in the courtroom began to shift. The temperature dropped. The starfield ceiling swirled, coalescing into a single point of brilliant white light. It descended slowly, taking shape as it came.

The Divine Witness was beautiful in the way a glacier was beautiful. Ancient, cold, and utterly indifferent to human suffering. It appeared as a woman carved from crystal, her features sharp and precise, her eyes holding the depth of eternity. Light refracted through her form, casting rainbows across the obsidian floor.

"I am called," she said, her voice resonating in multiple harmonics. "Who seeks the truth?"

"I do," the hero said, stepping forward. "I, Master Chen Wei of the Celestial Sword Sect, invoke the Divine Witness to expose the truth of the Demon Empress."

The crystal woman turned her gaze to Elara. "You have consented to my presence?"

"I have."

"Then let us begin." The Divine Witness raised her hand, and a circle of light formed around Elara's feet. "I will ask three questions. You must answer truthfully. Any deception will be instantly revealed, and the consequences will be... severe."

Elara's heart hammered against her ribs, but she kept her face neutral. "Understood."

"First question." The Divine Witness's voice sharpened. "Are you the Demon Empress, ruler of the Shadow Flame Palace, destroyer of the Azure Lotus Sect, and architect of the Massacre at Moonfall Valley?"

The words hit like physical blows. *The Massacre at Moonfall Valley.* The original Demon Empress's greatest atrocity. Three hundred cultivators, slaughtered in a single night.

*I can't answer this truthfully. If I say no, she'll know I'm an imposter. If I say yes, I'm admitting to mass murder.*

But the light was already pulsing around her feet, the Divine Witness's eyes boring into her soul. She had no time to think.

"I am the one currently inhabiting the body of the Demon Empress," Elara said carefully. "I carry her name, her title, and her responsibilities. Whether I am *her* is a matter of philosophical debate."

The Divine Witness tilted her head. "That is not a direct answer."

"It's the truth," Elara said. "You asked if I am the Demon Empress. The answer is complicated. I am Elara Chen, a lawyer from another world, who woke up in this body three weeks ago. I have the Demon Empress's memories, but I did not commit her crimes. I am not the woman who ordered the massacre. I am the woman who inherited her mess."

Gasps rippled through the courtroom. The hero's face went white. The judges leaned forward, their ancient eyes sharp with interest.

"You admit to being a reincarnated soul?" the head judge asked.

"I'm standing in the presence of a being that compels absolute truth," Elara said. "Lying would be pointless. Yes, I am a reincarnated soul. I died in my world, and I woke up here. I've been playing catch-up ever since."

The Divine Witness's light flickered. "Your answer is... acceptable. It contains no falsehood, though it dances around the edges of the question. I will allow it."

*One question down. Two to go.*

The hero recovered quickly, his expression hardening. "This changes nothing. Even if you're a reincarnated soul, you're still using the Demon Empress's body. You still have her power, her resources, her allies. You are her, legally and spiritually."

"Actually, the legal precedent is unclear," Elara said. "In my world, identity is determined by consciousness, not physical form. If I have a different soul, a different mind, and a different moral compass, then legally speaking, I am not responsible for the actions of the previous occupant of this body."

"This is not your world," the hero snapped.

"No, it's not. But I'm asking this court to apply the principles of justice, not vengeance." She turned to the judges. "You are celestial beings. You understand the nature of souls and reincarnation. Surely you can see the difference between me and the woman who wore this body before me."

The judges exchanged glances. The one in the center, a woman with silver hair and eyes like dying stars, spoke for the first time. "The distinction is noted. We will consider it when rendering judgment."

The Divine Witness raised her hand again. "Second question. What is your current cultivation level?"

Elara felt the blood drain from her face. *Of course. He's trying to expose my weakness.*

"I have no cultivation," she said.

The silence was absolute.

"I have no cultivation," she repeated. "The original Demon Empress was a peak-level cultivator, but when I took over this body, her cultivation was already sealed. I've been unable to access any of her power. I am, for all intents and purposes, a mortal."

The hero's face contorted with disbelief. "That's impossible. The Demon Empress was one of the most powerful beings in the cultivation world. How could her cultivation simply... disappear?"

"I don't know," Elara said. "I've been trying to figure that out myself. But the fact remains: I can't cultivate, I can't use qi, and I can't defend myself with any kind of supernatural power. The only weapons I have are my mind and my mouth."

The Divine Witness's light pulsed steadily. "She speaks the truth. This one has no cultivation. Not a single dantian of qi."

Murmurs erupted throughout the courtroom. Some sounded shocked, others gleeful. The hero's eyes glittered with calculation.

"If she has no cultivation," he said slowly, "then she's defenseless. She can't be the Demon Empress we feared. She's just... a woman."

"Correction," the Arbiter's voice rang out. "She's a woman who has managed to survive three weeks in the most hostile environment imaginable, without cultivation, without allies, and without power. That's not weakness. That's terrifying resourcefulness."

The hero ignored her. "Third question," he said to the Divine Witness. "Ask her about the Nightshade Conspiracy."

Elara's blood ran cold. *The Nightshade Conspiracy.* It was a fragment of memory she'd found buried deep in the Demon Empress's mind, a plan so dark and terrible that even the original Empress had sealed it away.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Elara said.

The hero smiled. "The Divine Witness will know if you're lying."

The crystal woman turned to Elara, her eyes piercing. "Third question. What do you know of the Nightshade Conspiracy?"

Elara's mind raced. She *did* know something. A fragment of memory. A shadow of a plan. But it wasn't complete, and she didn't understand its implications.

"I know that it exists," she said carefully. "I have fragments of memory from the original Demon Empress. She was involved in something called the Nightshade Conspiracy, but I don't know the details. I haven't been able to access those memories fully."

"Is that the truth?" the hero demanded.

The Divine Witness's light flickered. "She speaks the truth. She has incomplete knowledge of the Nightshade Conspiracy. She does not know its full scope."

The hero's smile widened. "Incomplete knowledge. But she *does* have knowledge. Which means she's still connected to the original Empress's plans. She's not as innocent as she claims."

"Having fragments of memory doesn't make me responsible for a conspiracy I didn't create," Elara said.

"It makes you a liability," the hero countered. "The Nightshade Conspiracy was designed to destroy the Celestial Order itself. If those fragments resurface, if you remember the full plan, you could bring about the end of everything we've built."

The judges leaned back, their faces unreadable. The head judge raised her hand. "The Divine Witness has spoken. We have heard the truth. Now we must decide what to do with it."

"Wait," Elara said. "I have a question of my own."

The judges exchanged glances. "This is not an interrogation," the head judge said. "You are the accused, not the prosecutor."

"Article 14 of the Celestial Code," Elara said, "grants the accused the right to call one witness of their own. I invoke that right now."

The Arbiter's voice drifted down, tinged with surprise. "She's correct. The accused may call one witness."

"Who do you wish to call?" the head judge asked.

Elara turned to the hero. "I call Master Chen Wei of the Celestial Sword Sect."

The hero's eyes widened. "What? You can't—"

"The Divine Witness compels truth from everyone in its presence," Elara said. "Including you."

The crystal woman's light pulsed. "She is correct. Once summoned, I compel truth from all who stand before me. Master Chen Wei, you will answer her questions truthfully."

The hero's face went pale. "I refuse."

"You can't refuse," Elara said. "The Divine Witness's authority supersedes your objections. Unless you have something to hide?"

The courtroom fell silent. Every eye turned to the hero.

"Master Chen Wei," the head judge said slowly, "you will submit to questioning. This court will not tolerate obstruction of justice."

The hero's jaw clenched. For a moment, Elara saw something flicker in his eyes. Fear.

*What is he hiding?*

"Fine," he said through gritted teeth. "Ask your questions."

Elara stepped forward, her mind sharpening. She had three questions, just as the Divine Witness had given her. She had to make them count.

"Master Chen Wei," she said, "did you know that the original Demon Empress was innocent of the Massacre at Moonfall Valley?"

The hero's face went white. "I... I don't—"

"The truth," the Divine Witness said, her voice echoing. "Speak the truth."

The hero's hands trembled. "Yes," he said, his voice barely a whisper. "I knew."

The courtroom erupted.

title: The Divine Revelation
content:
The courtroom fell silent as the Arbiter's manifestation solidified between the prosecution and defense tables. She was no longer a mere scroll-wielding figure—now she stood seven feet tall, her form shifting between human and something far older, her eyes holding galaxies.

"The Divine Witness has been summoned," she announced, her voice carrying harmonics that made Elara's teeth ache. "Once invoked, it cannot be dismissed until the truth has been fully revealed. All parties present shall speak only truth, conceal nothing, and bear their souls before the Celestial Order."

Elara's fingers dug into her palms. *Think. You're a lawyer. There's always a loophole.*

"Objection," she said, her voice steady despite the tremor in her chest. "The Divine Witness violates my right against self-incrimination under Article 17 of the Celestial Arbitration Code."

The head judge raised an eyebrow. "There is no such article."

"Then I'm invoking Article 17 as a precedent from the Mortal Realm Treaty of 892, which specifically states that no soul shall be compelled to testify against itself in any celestial proceeding." Elara had no idea if such a treaty existed. She was gambling on the fact that celestial bureaucracy was as convoluted as mortal bureaucracy.

The judges leaned together, whispering. The hero's smirk faltered.

"The treaty you reference," the head judge said slowly, "governs disputes between mortal and celestial entities. It does not apply to internal celestial matters."

"Then we need to establish jurisdiction," Elara countered. "I am currently inhabiting a mortal body—the body of the Demon Empress, yes, but still a mortal vessel. Under Article 3 of the same treaty, any being with mortal blood retains mortal rights in celestial proceedings."

The Arbiter's form flickered. "She's not wrong."

The hero slammed his hand on the prosecutor's table. "She's manipulating the system! This is exactly what the Demon Empress would do—twist words, exploit loopholes, avoid accountability!"

"Actually," Elara said, turning to face him fully, "this is what a *lawyer* does. You wanted a trial. Trials have rules. You don't get to break them just because you don't like my arguments."

Murmurs rippled through the cloud-walled courtroom. The spectators—cultivators from a dozen sects, celestial bureaucrats, and what appeared to be several floating eyeballs belonging to ancient beings—leaned forward with renewed interest.

The head judge raised a hand. Silence fell.

"Very well. The Divine Witness shall be modified. It will compel truth regarding the specific charges brought before this court—the crimes attributed to the Demon Empress in her capacity as ruler of the Nether Dominion. All other truths shall remain veiled."

Elara's shoulders relaxed a fraction. *I bought myself a shield. But it's paper-thin.*

The Arbiter's form began to glow. Light poured from her chest, coalescing into a sphere of pure radiance that floated to the center of the courtroom. It pulsed like a heartbeat, and with each pulse, Elara felt something tug at her consciousness—a gentle but insistent pressure, like a hand reaching for secrets she'd buried.

"Let the trial commence," the Arbiter intoned.

The hero rose, his robes flowing with practiced elegance. He was handsome in a way that felt manufactured, every feature sculpted to perfection. But his eyes—those eyes held a sharpness that Belen's had never possessed. This was a man who had spent years planning, waiting, preparing.

"May I approach the witness?" he asked.

"The defendant is not a witness," Elara said automatically. "She is the accused. She cannot be compelled to testify."

The hero's smile returned. "I'm not calling the defendant. I'm calling the first witness." He turned to the judges. "The Celestial Sword Sect calls forth the soul of Elder Ming, former advisor to the Demon Empress."

Elara's blood turned to ice.

A figure materialized in the witness box—an old man with a long white beard and robes that had once been grand but now hung in tattered shreds. His eyes were vacant, his form translucent. A soul pulled from whatever afterlife he'd been consigned to.

*He knows everything,* Elara thought. *He knows I'm not her.*

Elder Ming's gaze found her, and for a moment, something like recognition flickered in his dead eyes. Then it was gone, replaced by the hollow obedience of a summoned spirit.

"Elder Ming," the hero began, circling the witness box, "you served the Demon Empress for three hundred years. Is that correct?"

"Yes." The voice was a whisper, carried on winds that didn't exist.

"And in all that time, you witnessed her rise to power, her consolidation of the Nether Dominion, and her various... acquisitions."

"I did."

"Tell us," the hero said, stopping directly in front of the spirit, "about the Night of Crimson Petals."

The courtroom went still. Even the floating eyeballs stopped moving.

Elara's mind raced. She'd read about the Night of Crimson Petals in the memories she'd inherited—fragments of a massacre that had wiped out three noble families who had opposed the Demon Empress's ascension. Thousands had died. Children. Elders. Innocents caught in the crossfire.

And the real Demon Empress had orchestrated every death.

"The Demon Empress," Elder Ming said, his voice gaining strength, "invited the heads of the three families to a banquet. She served them wine laced with soul-destroying poison. While they died, she ordered their compounds razed. No survivors."

"Did she express any remorse?"

"None. She watched from her palace balcony as the flames consumed them. She laughed."

Elara closed her eyes. *That wasn't me. But it was this body. These hands.*

"Your honor," she said, opening her eyes, "I object. The witness is testifying to actions taken by the previous occupant of this body. I am not the Demon Empress. I am a separate soul bound to this vessel through circumstances I did not choose."

The head judge's expression was unreadable. "The Celestial Order recognizes that souls may be bound to bodies they did not originally inhabit. However, the body itself carries karma. The vessel you occupy committed these crimes. Until proven otherwise, you bear that karma."

"That's guilt by association," Elara snapped. "In mortal law—"

"This is not mortal law."

The hero's smile had returned, wider than before. "Elder Ming, one more question. In the months before the Demon Empress's planned execution, did you notice any change in her behavior?"

Elara's heart stopped.

Elder Ming's vacant eyes turned toward her. "Yes. She became... different. Her mannerisms changed. Her speech patterns shifted. She asked questions she should have known the answers to. She showed mercy where she had never shown mercy before."

"Mercy?" The hero's voice dripped with mock surprise. "The Demon Empress showed mercy?"

"To a servant who spilled tea on her robes. She dismissed the girl instead of executing her."

"And when did this change occur?"

"After her cultivation collapsed. When she became mortal."

The hero turned to face the judges, his arms spread wide. "The Demon Empress's cultivation collapsed mysteriously. Her personality changed dramatically. And now she claims to be a different soul entirely." He let the words hang in the air. "Convenient, isn't it?"

Elara's mind was screaming. *He's setting me up. He's going to claim I faked everything—the soul transfer, the memory loss, the cultivation collapse. He's going to argue that this is all an elaborate deception to escape justice.*

"Alternatively," the hero continued, his voice dropping, "the Demon Empress found a way to transfer her consciousness into a mortal soul, allowing her to claim immunity while retaining her true nature. A perfect crime."

The courtroom erupted.

The head judge slammed a gavel made of crystallized lightning. "Order!"

The noise died down, but the tension remained, thick enough to choke on.

"Prosecutor," the head judge said, "do you have evidence to support this theory?"

"I do." The hero reached into his robes and produced a scroll—ancient, cracked, sealed with black wax. "This is a record from the Forbidden Archives. It details a ritual known as Soul Veil Transfer, a technique developed by the Demon Empress's ancestors. It allows a cultivator to swap souls with a mortal, abandoning their cultivation and assuming the mortal's identity while retaining their memories and consciousness."

Elara's stomach dropped.

"The ritual," the hero continued, "requires the mortal to be willing. Or"—he paused for effect—"the mortal must be at the point of death, their soul weakened enough to be displaced."

*He knows.*

The hero locked eyes with her, and for a moment, she saw triumph blazing in his gaze. "I believe the Demon Empress found a dying mortal—a human lawyer, recently deceased—and used the Soul Veil Transfer to escape her execution. She swapped bodies, left her cultivation behind, and now stands before this court pretending to be innocent."

"That's insane," Elara said, but her voice came out weaker than she intended.

"Is it?" The hero walked toward her, stopping inches away. "Then prove it. Submit to the Divine Witness. Let it see your true soul. If you're truly a mortal lawyer named Elara Chen, it will confirm your story."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you confirm my theory."

Elara looked at the judges. At the Arbiter. At the floating sphere of light that pulsed with terrible knowing.

She was trapped.

If she refused, they'd assume the hero was right—that she was the Demon Empress in disguise, playing games to save her own skin. They'd annihilate her without a second thought.

If she submitted, the Divine Witness would reveal everything. Her past life. Her death. Her arrival in this body. And when it did, the hero would argue that she was still the Demon Empress—just in a new container.

Either way, she lost.

*Unless...*

Elara took a breath. She'd spent her career finding loopholes in contracts that had been reviewed by a dozen lawyers. She'd found escape clauses in documents that had been notarized by supreme courts. She'd turned certain defeat into victory more times than she could count.

And this was just another contract.

"I'll submit," she said.

The hero's eyes widened. "You—"

"On one condition." Elara turned to the judges. "The Divine Witness must confirm not just my identity, but also the identity of the prosecutor."

The hero's face went pale. "What?"

"You heard me." Elara's voice was steady now, her fear transmuted into cold calculation. "If we're going to dig into souls, let's dig into everyone's. I want to know exactly who is prosecuting me. I want to know his true name, his true nature, and"—she paused, letting the silence build—"I want to know if he has any personal connection to the crimes of the Demon Empress."

"He doesn't," the hero said quickly. Too quickly.

"Then you have nothing to worry about." Elara smiled—the same smile she'd used to close billion-dollar deals. "Unless, of course, you do."

The judges exchanged glances. The head judge's expression was unreadable.

"The defendant's request is unusual," the Arbiter said slowly, "but not without precedent. If one party submits to the Divine Witness, all parties must submit equally."

"Objection!" the hero shouted. "I am not the accused!"

"You are a party to these proceedings," the Arbiter replied. "The Divine Witness does not discriminate."

The hero's face cycled through a dozen emotions in the span of a second. Fear. Anger. Panic. And then—something else.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Witness of Truth

The celestial courtroom held its breath. Elara's mind raced through possibilities, objections, escape routes—each one slamming shut like a prison door.

"The Divine Witness," the head judge repeated, stroking a beard made of starlight. "That is... an unusual request for a preliminary hearing."

"The accused invoked a jury," the hero said smoothly. "I'm merely exercising my reciprocal rights under the Celestial Code of Procedure."

*Code of Procedure.* The words triggered something in Elara's memory—a dusty volume in her law firm's library, written in a language she'd never learned, that she'd used as a doorstop for years. *How did I know that?*

She pushed the thought aside. "I object," she said, her voice steady despite the panic clawing at her chest. "The Divine Witness is a coercive mechanism that violates the accused's right to—"

"To what?" the hero interrupted. "To hide the truth? To conceal your crimes behind legal technicalities?"

"I was going to say 'to present evidence in a controlled manner.'" Elara turned to the judges. "The Divine Witness doesn't distinguish between relevant and irrelevant truths. It exposes everything—thoughts, memories, intentions. That's not justice. That's psychic vivisection."

The three judges conferred in whispers that sounded like wind through bones.

"She has a point," the female judge said. Her voice carried the weight of mountains.

"The prosecutor has rights too," the male judge countered. His eyes burned like dying stars.

The third judge—neither male nor female, neither young nor old—remained silent, watching Elara with an expression that made her feel like a specimen under glass.

"We will allow the Divine Witness," the head judge announced, "but with restrictions. The witness will only answer questions directly relevant to the charges. No fishing expeditions."

The hero's smile faltered. "That's—"

"Non-negotiable." The head judge raised a hand. "The accused's objection has merit. We will not allow this court to become an instrument of cosmic voyeurism."

*Interesting,* Elara thought. *The judges have their own agenda.*

She filed that information away for later use.

"Bring forth the Divine Witness," the head judge commanded.

The courtroom dimmed. The stars on the ceiling flickered and died, one by one, until only darkness remained. Then, from the center of the room, light began to grow—not warm light, not cold light, but light that seemed to *see* through everything it touched.

The Divine Witness took shape.

It had no form Elara could describe. It was a column of radiance, a pillar of awareness, a presence that pressed against her consciousness like fingers probing a wound. She felt exposed, naked, her thoughts laid bare.

*No,* she told herself. *I'm still in control. I'm still me.*

But was she? Was she Elara Chen, corporate lawyer? Or was she the Demon Empress, whose body she'd stolen? Or was she something else entirely—something that had been growing in the space between identities?

"I am the Witness," the being said. Its voice was not sound but understanding, words that bypassed her ears and planted themselves directly in her mind. "I see all truths. I know all secrets. I speak only what is."

The hero stepped forward. "Divine Witness, I ask you this: Does the accused possess the cultivation she claims to have lost?"

The Witness turned its attention to Elara. She felt its gaze like a scalpel, cutting through layers of deception she hadn't even known she'd built.

"The accused possesses no cultivation," the Witness said. "Her meridians are empty. Her dantian is sealed. She has the spiritual capacity of a mortal."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. The hero's eyes narrowed.

"Then how did she survive the execution?" he pressed. "How did she heal from wounds that should have killed her?"

"That question is not relevant to the charges," the female judge interjected.

"Let the Witness answer," the head judge said. "It will help establish context."

The Witness turned its attention to Elara again. She felt it delving deeper, searching for something she didn't know she was hiding.

"The accused survived through means unrelated to cultivation," the Witness said. "A bargain was struck. A price was paid."

"What bargain?" the hero demanded. "With whom?"

"The question exceeds the bounds of relevance," the male judge said. "The Witness has answered enough."

But the Witness was already speaking. "The bargain was made with—"

The Witness stopped.

For the first time, it seemed uncertain. Its light flickered, dimmed, then flared brighter than before.

"The bargain was made with a power that is not named in this realm," the Witness said. "A power that is older than the stars and younger than the morning dew. A power that—"

"Enough," the head judge commanded. "The Witness has answered the relevant question. The accused has no cultivation. The matter is settled."

But Elara saw the way the judges exchanged glances. She saw the way the hero's hands clenched at his sides. She saw the way the crowd leaned forward, hungry for more.

*They know something,* she thought. *They know what that bargain was. And they're afraid.*

The Witness's light faded, but its presence remained—a constant pressure at the edge of Elara's consciousness, like a door that had been left slightly ajar.

"Now," the head judge said, "we will proceed with the presentation of evidence. Prosecutor, you may begin."

The hero stepped forward, a scroll appearing in his hands. "The Celestial Sword Sect presents the following evidence: Witness testimony from seventeen cultivators who saw the Demon Empress commit acts of murder, torture, and spiritual corruption. Physical evidence in the form of blood-stained artifacts, destroyed cultivation tools, and—"

"Objection," Elara said. "Hearsay."

The hero blinked. "What?"

"Hearsay," she repeated. "Seventeen witnesses who *saw* the Demon Empress commit crimes. But did any of them actually witness the crimes, or did they merely hear about them from others? Were they present at the scenes, or did they arrive afterward?"

"Your point is irrelevant to celestial law," the hero said.

"Actually," the Arbiter's voice echoed from above, "it's not. The Celestial Code of Procedure, Article 7, Section C: 'Testimony must be based on direct observation. Second-hand accounts are inadmissible.' "

The hero's face reddened. "These cultivators were present at the scenes!"

"Were they?" Elara asked. "Let's test that. Call your first witness."

The head judge nodded. "So be it. Summon the first witness."

A portal opened beside the prosecutor's table, and a man stepped through. He was middle-aged, with the bearing of a seasoned cultivator and the eyes of someone who had seen terrible things.

"State your name and affiliation," the hero said.

"Zhang Wei. Former elder of the Falling Leaf Sect."

"And what did you witness on the night of the Crimson Moon?"

Zhang Wei's face tightened. "I saw the Demon Empress destroy our sect's ancestral hall. She killed twelve elders and scattered their souls to the winds."

"Objection," Elara said. "Leading the witness."

The hero's jaw clenched. "This is standard procedure."

"In mortal courts, yes. But this is a celestial arbitration." Elara turned to the judges. "The prosecutor is feeding the witness his testimony. Let the witness speak without prompting."

The head judge considered this. "Sustained. Witness, describe what you saw in your own words."

Zhang Wei looked confused. "I... I saw the Demon Empress. She was standing in the ruins of the ancestral hall. There was blood on her hands."

"Did you see her kill anyone?" Elara asked.

"I saw the bodies."

"That's not what I asked. Did you *see* her commit murder?"

Zhang Wei hesitated. "The hall was in flames. There was chaos. I saw her standing over the bodies of the elders, but..."

"But what?"

"I was outside the hall. There were others there too—disciples, servants, guards. Anyone could have..."

"Could have what?"

"Could have killed them." Zhang Wei's voice dropped. "The Demon Empress was powerful. She didn't need to get her hands dirty. But that night, she was covered in blood. It didn't make sense."

The courtroom stirred. The hero's expression darkened.

"Witness," Elara said, "you said the Demon Empress was powerful. How powerful?"

"Peak Nascent Soul stage. One of the strongest cultivators in the realm."

"And yet she got her hands dirty? She personally killed twelve elders when she could have destroyed them with a thought?"

Zhang Wei's eyes widened. "I... I never thought about that."

"Because it doesn't make sense," Elara said. "Because the Demon Empress wouldn't have needed to personally kill anyone. She could have annihilated your entire sect with a wave of her hand. So why would she get her hands dirty?"

"I don't know."

"Could it be that someone else killed those elders and framed the Demon Empress?"

"I... it's possible." Zhang Wei looked at the hero, then at Elara. "I never considered that possibility."

"Thank you, witness." Elara turned to the judges. "I move to dismiss this testimony as unreliable."

The hero slammed his hand on the table. "You can't just—"

"The accused has raised valid concerns," the female judge said. "The testimony is noted but given reduced weight. Continue, prosecutor."

The hero's eyes burned with fury. He summoned another witness, then another. Every time, Elara found holes in their testimony—inconsistencies, gaps, possibilities of alternative explanations.

But with each witness, she felt the Divine Witness's gaze growing heavier. It was watching her, measuring her, waiting for the moment when she would slip.

*I can't keep this up forever,* she thought. *Eventually, I'll run out of objections.*

The final witness was a young woman, barely more than a girl. Her eyes were hollow, her hands trembling.

"State your name," the hero said.

"Li Mei. Former disciple of the Celestial Sword Sect."

"And what did you see?"

Li Mei's voice was barely a whisper. "I saw the Demon Empress kill my master. She... she tore out his heart and ate it."

The courtroom gasped. Even the judges leaned forward.

"Describe what you saw," the hero pressed.

"She was laughing. She said that his cultivation would make her stronger. She said that she would devour the entire sect. And then she... she *changed*. Her eyes turned red. Her teeth grew sharp. She wasn't human anymore."

Elara felt the Divine Witness's gaze intensify. It was probing her, searching for the truth beneath the lies.

*I don't remember this,* she thought. *I don't remember any of this.*

But the Demon Empress did. The memories were there, buried beneath layers of borrowed identity—memories of blood and screams and pleasure.

*No,* Elara told herself. *Those aren't my memories. I'm not her. I'm not—*

"You're lying," the hero said.

Everyone froze.

"Prosecutor?" the head judge asked. "Explain yourself."

The hero pointed at Li Mei. "She's lying. I know because I was there. And the Demon Empress didn't kill her master."

Li Mei's face went white. "What? No, I saw—"

"You saw what you were meant to see." The hero turned to the judges. "Li Mei's master was killed by someone else. Someone who wore the Demon Empress's face, who used her signature techniques, who made sure she was blamed."

"Who?" the head judge demanded.

The hero's

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Witness of Truth

The courtroom held its breath. Elara's mind raced through every legal loophole, every procedural objection, every delaying tactic she'd ever used. But this wasn't a mortal court. The rules here were written by beings who had seen civilizations rise and fall like morning dew.

"Your Excellencies," Elara said, her voice steady despite the cold dread spreading through her chest, "I must object. The Divine Witness is an extraordinary measure. Protocol requires that I be informed of such requests before the proceedings begin, with adequate time to prepare a response."

The head judge's ancient eyes flickered with something that might have been amusement. "And you know our protocols, mortal?"

"I know the contract I signed." Elara pulled the Arbiter's scroll from her sleeve. "Article 7, section C: 'All parties must be given equal opportunity to prepare for any evidentiary proceedings.' I was not given that opportunity."

The hero—she needed to learn his actual name, she realized—laughed. "Clever. But the Divine Witness isn't evidence. It's a witness. Different classification."

"The classification is irrelevant." Elara turned to face him fully. "The intent of the law is to ensure fair proceedings. Calling a being that can compel absolute truth without warning is the opposite of fair. It's an ambush."

"Objection noted," the head judge said. "Overruled."

*Damn.*

"The Divine Witness is already bound to this realm," the judge continued. "It arrived the moment the prosecutor made his request. The question is not whether it will appear, but whether you will submit to its testimony."

Elara's hands trembled, but she kept them clasped behind her back. "And if I refuse?"

"Then your objection is noted, and the proceedings continue without it. But..." The judge leaned forward. "Your refusal will be taken into consideration. If you have nothing to hide, why would you fear the truth?"

*Because the truth will get me killed.*

She looked at the hero. He was watching her with that same calculating respect from before. He knew something. Not everything, but enough to suspect that the Demon Empress he'd come to execute was not the same woman who now stood in this courtroom.

"The divine witness," Elara said slowly, "compels truth. But truth is subjective. What one being sees as truth, another might interpret differently. Can the witness distinguish between objective fact and subjective belief?"

The Arbiter's voice echoed from above. "The Divine Witness of Truth perceives all layers of reality simultaneously. It sees your actions, your intentions, your memories, and your soul. There is no distinction between objective and subjective. All is truth."

*Great. So much for that argument.*

"Then I have one condition," Elara said.

The hero raised an eyebrow. "You're in no position to make demands."

"Actually, I am." Elara turned to the judges. "The contract states that I have the right to challenge any evidence presented against me. The Divine Witness is, by the prosecutor's own admission, a witness. Therefore, I have the right to cross-examine it."
