The hero's eyes met hers. "I request the Divine Witness not to expose her crimes—those are already documented. I request it to expose her *true nature*. Because only then can we decide if she deserves a trial, or if she deserves immediate annihilation."

He sat down. The courtroom was silent.

"Ms. Chen," the head judge said. "Your opening statement."

Elara stood. She'd prepared a dozen different speeches, but now, standing under the celestial lights, she threw them all out.

"I'm going to tell you something that will make you hate me," she said. "And then I'm going to tell you something that will make you question everything you believe."

She walked to the center of the room. "I *am* a usurper. I'm not the Demon Empress. I'm a lawyer from a world without cultivation, without magic, without any of the things you consider sacred. I died at my desk, overworked and underpaid, and woke up in a body that was about to be executed."

The crowd's reaction was immediate—gasps, whispers, outrage.

"And I have no idea why I'm here."

She let that sink in.

"I didn't choose this. I didn't scheme to possess this body. I was thrown into a nightmare and told to survive. And survive I did. Not through cultivation, not through magic, but through *intelligence*. Through *law*. Through the same tools that built your precious Celestial Order."

She pointed at the hero. "He wants you to believe I'm a threat. But ask yourselves—what have I actually done since I arrived? I've followed every rule. I've respected every protocol. I've used the system exactly as it was designed."

"And yet," she continued, "he's afraid. Because I'm winning. I'm beating him at his own game. And he can't stand it."

She turned to the judges. "Call your Divine Witness. I have nothing to hide. I'm exactly what I appear to be—a woman trying to survive in a world that wants her dead. The only question is whether this court values truth or vengeance."

She sat down. Her heart was pounding so hard she thought it might burst.

But she'd done it. She'd controlled the narrative.

Now she just had to survive the truth.

---

The summoning of the Divine Witness took three hours.

Three hours of chanting, of ritual circles being drawn on the obsidian floor, of celestial energy building until the air itself crackled with power. Elara watched, fascinated despite herself, as the being materialized.

It wasn't what she expected.

No blinding light. No terrifying visage. The Divine Witness appeared as a woman—ordinary, unassuming, with kind eyes and graying hair. She could have been anyone's grandmother.

"I am the Truth," she said, her voice soft. "And I see all that is hidden."

She looked directly at Elara. "You are afraid."

"Yes," Elara said. No point in lying.

"Good. Fear keeps the truth sharp." The Divine Witness turned to the judges. "I am ready. Who shall I examine first?"

"The defendant," the hero said immediately.

"Very well."

The Divine Witness walked toward Elara, each step echoing in the silent room. She stopped inches away, close enough that Elara could smell incense and old paper.

"Look into my eyes," she said. "And speak no lies."

Elara met her gaze. The world tilted.

Suddenly she was *everywhere* and *nowhere*. She felt her own memories rushing past—her corporate office, the endless contracts, the night she died. Then the Demon Empress's memories—centuries of cruelty, of power, of loneliness. Both sets of memories, both identities, tangled together until she couldn't tell where one ended and the other began.

"Elara Chen," the Divine Witness said, and her voice echoed in Elara's skull. "Were you the one who orchestrated the massacre at Jade Valley?"

"No."

The word came out before she could stop it. And it was true. The Demon Empress had done it, but Elara hadn't.

"Did you knowingly corrupt the sacred springs of the Celestial Sword Sect?"

"No."

Another truth. The Demon Empress's crime, not hers.

"Did you kill the High Elder of the Phoenix Clan?"

"No."

The crowd was murmuring again. The hero's face had gone pale.

"Have you committed any crime since inhabiting this body?"

Elara paused. The Divine Witness's eyes bore into her, and she felt the truth rising like bile.

"I manipulated the legal system," she said. "I used loopholes. I exploited technicalities. I did everything I could to avoid execution."

"That's not a crime," the Divine Witness said. "That's strategy."

"Then no. I haven't committed any crimes."

The Divine Witness turned to the judges. "She speaks the truth. Every word. She is not responsible for the Demon Empress's crimes, and she has committed no new offenses since her arrival."

The courtroom erupted.

"This is impossible!" the hero shouted. "She's lying—the Divine Witness must be flawed!"

"The Divine Witness is never flawed," the head judge said coldly. "But perhaps your understanding of justice is."

Elara allowed herself a small smile. She'd done it. She'd—

"Wait," the Divine Witness said.

Everyone froze.

"There is something else. Something buried so deep that even she forgot it."

The Divine Witness turned back to Elara, and her eyes were no longer kind. They were ancient, terrible, full of stars and void.

"Elara Chen. You were not chosen at random. You were *sent*."

Elara's blood went cold. "What?"

"Your death. Your rebirth. It was not an accident. It was not fate. It was *design*."

"By whom?"

The Divine Witness opened her mouth to answer—and screamed.

Her body convulsed. Light poured from her eyes, her mouth, her ears. The celestial courtroom shook. The judges rose to their feet, their ancient faces twisted in alarm.

"Something is blocking me," the Divine Witness gasped. "Something powerful. Something that doesn't want the truth revealed."

"Then reveal it anyway," Elara said. "I need to know."

The Divine Witness's eyes met hers one last time. "The one who sent you... is me."

The courtroom went dark.

When the light returned, the Divine Witness was gone.

And in her place stood a figure Elara recognized.

The Arbiter.

"I'm sorry," the Arbiter said. "But you weren't supposed to find out yet."

She raised her hand, and the world dissolved.

*No,* Elara thought, reaching out. *No, I was so close.*

The last thing she saw before everything went black was the hero's face—and for the first time, he looked genuinely afraid.

title: The Witness of Truth
content:
The courtroom held its breath. Elara's mind raced through possibilities, each one collapsing faster than the last. Under the Divine Witness, she couldn't lie about her identity. She couldn't hide her lack of cultivation. She couldn't conceal that she was a corporate lawyer from another world who had somehow ended up in the body of a demon empress.

But if she refused, she'd look guilty. The judges would assume she had something to hide. And in a celestial court, presumption of guilt was the default.

"I accept," Elara said.

The hero's smile faltered. He'd expected her to fight, to stall, to find some loophole. Instead, she'd agreed.

"Are you certain?" the Arbiter's voice asked, a rare note of concern creeping through. "Once summoned, the Divine Witness cannot be dismissed. It will see everything."

"I'm certain." Elara turned to face the judges. "But I have conditions."

The head judge raised an eyebrow. "Conditions?"

"First, I want full immunity for anything the Divine Witness reveals about my past that is not directly related to the charges against me. I don't want my personal history used as ammunition."

"Reasonable," the head judge said. "Granted."

"Second, the hero—" She pointed at him. "—subjects himself to the same examination. If we're talking about truth, let's talk about *all* truth."

The hero's face went pale. "That's not—"

"Accepted," the head judge said, cutting him off. "The Divine Witness does not discriminate."

Elara smiled. "Then I'm ready."

The Arbiter descended from the ceiling, her form shifting from ethereal mist into solid, imposing presence. She raised her hands, and the starfield above them began to swirl.

"By the ancient compact," the Arbiter intoned, "by the blood of the first judges, by the unbreakable laws that bind heaven and earth—I summon the Divine Witness of Truth."

The stars spun faster. The cloud walls trembled. And then the light came.

It was not a being. It was not a voice. It was simply *presence*—a weight that pressed down on everything, that peeled back layers of self-deception and comfortable lies. Elara felt it settle over her like a second skin, warm and cold at the same time.

*You are not afraid.*

The thought came unbidden. It was not her own.

"You can hear my thoughts," Elara said.

*I can hear everything. Every truth. Every lie. Every secret you've buried so deep you've forgotten it yourself.*

The head judge cleared his throat. "Let us begin. The Divine Witness will question the accused first."

The presence shifted, focusing entirely on Elara. She felt exposed, raw, like every wall she'd built was being dismantled brick by brick.

*Who are you?*

The question was simple. Direct. And impossible to dodge.

"My name is Elara Chen," she said. "I died at thirty-four years old from cardiac arrest caused by overwork. I was a corporate lawyer at Sterling & Price. I woke up in the body of the Demon Empress three days before her execution."

Gasps rippled through the courtroom. The hero's eyes widened. Even the judges leaned forward.

*What happened to the original Demon Empress?*

"I don't know," Elara said. "I don't remember her memories. I don't have her cultivation. I don't know her plans or her allies or her enemies. I woke up in a cell with chains on my wrists and a death sentence hanging over my head."

More murmuring. The hero stood up.

"This is absurd," he said. "You expect us to believe that the Demon Empress was simply *replaced* by a mortal lawyer?"

"I don't expect you to believe anything," Elara replied. "But the Divine Witness is compelling the truth. You can feel it. I can't lie."

*She is not lying,* the Divine Witness confirmed. *Every word is truth.*

The courtroom went silent.

The hero sat down slowly, his expression unreadable. For a long moment, he just stared at her. Then he laughed—a short, bitter sound.

"All this time," he said. "All this time I was preparing to fight the most dangerous woman in the cultivation world. And instead, I'm facing a mortal with no cultivation and no allies."

"Don't underestimate me," Elara said. "I've won cases against Fortune 500 companies. I've negotiated treaties between warring nations. I've made grown men cry in depositions."

"Those are mortal achievements," the hero said. "They mean nothing here."

"Then let me prove it." Elara turned to the judges. "I request the floor to present my defense."

The head judge nodded. "You may proceed."

Elara walked to the center of the courtroom. The Divine Witness's presence still pressed down on her, but she was getting used to it. She straightened her spine, adjusted her sleeves, and began.

"The charges against me are as follows: first, conspiracy to overthrow the Celestial Order. Second, murder of Elder Wei of the Jade Phoenix Sect. Third, corruption of sacred artifacts. Fourth, unauthorized use of forbidden techniques. Fifth, harboring demons."

She paused, letting the list hang in the air.

"The prosecution claims I committed these crimes as the Demon Empress. But as the Divine Witness has established, I am *not* the Demon Empress. I am a mortal who woke up in her body three days ago. I could not have committed any of these crimes because I wasn't here."

"The body is the same," the hero argued. "The crimes were committed by the body you now inhabit."

"Criminal liability does not transfer with the body," Elara said. "If someone steals a car and then sells it to an innocent buyer, the buyer is not guilty of theft. If someone commits murder and then dies, their heir is not guilty of murder."

"That's mortal law," the hero said.

"Actually," the Arbiter interjected, "it's also celestial law. Article 47 of the Celestial Code clearly states that punishment is tied to the soul, not the vessel. If the soul is different, the vessel bears no guilt."

The hero's face fell. "But—"

"The Arbiter is correct," the head judge said. "This is well-established precedent."

Elara smiled. "Then the charges against me should be dismissed."

"Not so fast," the hero said. He turned to the judges. "Even if she is not guilty of the Demon Empress's crimes, she is still an unregistered soul in a borrowed body. She has no cultivation, no protection, and no right to be here. By celestial law, she should be returned to the mortal realm."

"Returned?" Elara asked. "You mean killed."

"I mean *returned*," the hero said. "Your soul does not belong in this world. You are a disruption to the natural order."

"He has a point," the head judge said. "The Celestial Order exists to maintain balance. An unregistered soul from another world threatens that balance."

"So does execution of an innocent person," Elara countered. "But more importantly, I didn't choose to come here. I didn't perform a ritual or make a deal. I simply *woke up* here. If the Celestial Order is so concerned about balance, shouldn't it investigate *how* I got here instead of punishing me for it?"

The judges exchanged glances.

"She's right," the Arbiter said. "The method of her arrival is more concerning than her presence. Someone or something brought her here. That someone or something should be found."

"Or," the hero said slowly, "she's lying about not knowing how she got here."

The Divine Witness pulsed.

*She is not lying.*

The hero's jaw tightened. "The Divine Witness can be fooled."

Now it was Elara's turn to laugh. "The Divine Witness that *you* requested? The one you claimed was infallible? Now you're saying it can be fooled?"

"I'm saying we don't know its limits."

"I know its limits," the Arbiter said coldly. "I have summoned it seventeen times in the last three thousand years. It has never been wrong. It has never been fooled. It cannot be deceived, bribed, or threatened."

The hero fell silent.

"Then the case is clear," the head judge said. "The accused is not guilty of the crimes of the Demon Empress. She is, however, an unregistered soul in a borrowed body. This court rules that she must be—"

"Wait," Elara said.

The head judge paused. "You have something to add?"

"I do." Elara took a breath. "I request asylum."

"Asylum?"

"By the laws of the Celestial Order, any soul that enters a realm without intent or malice may request asylum from persecution. Article 201, section A."

The hero's face went white. "You can't be serious."

"I'm completely serious." Elara turned to face the judges. "I didn't choose to come here. I didn't commit any crimes. But if I'm returned to the mortal realm, I will die—and not just die, but be *annihilated*. The contract the Arbiter showed me said so."

"She's right," the Arbiter said. "The original agreement stated that if she was found guilty, she would face annihilation. But she was found *not guilty*. The contract is void."

"Then she should be released," the hero said.

"Released into what?" Elara asked. "I have no cultivation. I have no allies. I have no idea how to survive in this world. If you release me, I'll be dead within a week."

"That's not our problem."

"It is your problem," Elara said, "because if you release an innocent person to die, you are complicit in their death. And that violation of celestial law carries a penalty of—"

"Enough," the head judge said. "We are not here to debate hypotheticals."

"Then grant my asylum request."

The judges exchanged glances. The head judge sighed.

"We cannot grant asylum without a sponsor," he said. "Someone must vouch for you. Someone must take responsibility for your actions and your presence in this realm."

Elara's heart sank. "Who?"

"Anyone of sufficient standing in the cultivation world. A sect leader, a clan head, a high-ranking official of the Celestial Order."

She looked around the courtroom. The faces staring back at her were hostile, suspicious, or indifferent. No one would sponsor her. No one would risk their reputation on a mortal with no cultivation.

"I'll do it."

The voice came from behind her. Elara turned.

The hero was standing, his expression conflicted but resolved.

"I'll sponsor her," he said again.

"Why?" Elara asked, genuinely confused.

"Because," the hero said, "you're not the Demon Empress. And I've been hunting the wrong person for three years."

He walked toward her, stopping a few feet away.

"I'm Li Wei," he said. "And I think we have a common enemy."

The courtroom erupted into chaos. The judges banged their gavels. The crowd shouted. Elara stood in the center of the storm, staring at the man who was supposed to kill her.

"What common enemy?" she asked.

Li Wei's eyes darkened. "The person who brought you here. The person who killed the real Demon Empress. The person who's been manipulating the Celestial Order from the shadows."

He leaned closer, his voice dropping to a whisper.

"Your predecessor was not a villain. She was a victim. And I think you and I are the only ones who can prove it."

The Divine Witness pulsed one last time.

*He is not lying.*

Elara's world tilted. Nothing was what it seemed. The hero was not a

title: The Witness's Verdict
content:
The courtroom held its breath. Elara felt every eye upon her, measuring, judging, weighing her worth. The hero's smile hadn't wavered, and she understood why—he'd just played the one card she couldn't counter.

*If I refuse the Divine Witness, I look guilty. If I accept, I'm exposed.*

Either way, she lost.

"The accused may respond," the head judge said, his voice carrying no hint of favor.

Elara's mind raced through legal precedent, both from her world and the fragmented memories of the Demon Empress she'd inherited. There had to be a loophole. There was always a loophole.

"I have a counter-request," she said slowly.

The hero's smile faltered.

"The Divine Witness compels truth," Elara continued, buying time to think. "But truth is subjective. What I believe to be true may differ from what another believes. The Witness can only compel *my* truth, not objective reality."

"That's..." the hero started.

"That's correct," the Arbiter's voice interrupted. "The Divine Witness reveals the truth as the speaker knows it. It does not reveal what *is*, only what *was perceived*."

*Thank you, metaphysical legal system.*

"However," Elara pressed on, "if the prosecutor wishes to invoke the Witness, I request equal access. I want to call witnesses of my own, and I want them bound by the same compulsion."

The judges conferred in whispers that sounded like thunder.

"Granted," the head judge said. "But know this—the Witness will remain for the duration of the trial. You cannot escape its gaze."

The hero sat down, his expression carefully neutral. But Elara saw his hands grip the table edge until his knuckles went white.

*Good. Let him worry.*

The courtroom rearranged itself. The obsidian floor rippled, and from its depths rose a pedestal of crystal. Within it swirled something that looked like liquid starlight, pulsing with a rhythm that matched Elara's heartbeat.

"The Divine Witness is present," the Arbiter announced. "Let the trial begin."

The head judge raised a hand. "First charge: The accused did knowingly and maliciously poison the water source of the Azure Lotus Sect, causing the deaths of three hundred and forty-seven cultivators."

*That's... new.*

Elara had read the Demon Empress's memories. She knew about the massacres, the betrayals, the cruelty. But this particular charge wasn't in the record. Either the prosecution had found something she didn't know about, or—

"Presenting Exhibit A," the hero said, producing a jade vial. "This poison was found in the Demon Empress's private chambers. It matches the toxin used in the attack."

A servant brought the vial to the judges. One of them uncorked it, sniffed, and recoiled.

"This is Soul-Breaking Venom," the judge said. "A poison so potent it can kill a cultivator's spirit before their body dies."

"And we have a witness," the hero continued, "who saw the Demon Empress purchasing this exact poison three days before the attack."

The doors at the back of the courtroom swung open. A merchant shuffled in, his face pale with terror. He was a mortal—Elara could tell by the way his energy flickered like a candle in wind.

"State your name and occupation," the hero said.

"Zhang Wei. I... I run an apothecary in the Shadow Market."

"And do you recognize the woman sitting at the defense table?"

Zhang Wei's eyes met Elara's. He trembled. "Y-yes. That's her. The Demon Empress."

"Did she purchase Soul-Breaking Venom from you?"

*Here it comes.*

Under the Divine Witness, Zhang Wei couldn't lie. But Elara had never visited his shop. She knew this from the Demon Empress's memories. The real Demon Empress had been arrogant, cruel, and murderous—but she'd never used poison. She considered it beneath her.

"Zhang Wei," the head judge said, "you are bound by the Witness. Speak only the truth."

The merchant's face contorted. Sweat beaded on his forehead. "I... I..."

The crystal pedestal pulsed. Light seared through the courtroom.

"I don't remember!" Zhang Wei screamed. "I don't remember who bought it! I've been trying to remember for months, but every time I get close, the memory disappears!"

*What?*

The hero shot to his feet. "That's impossible. The Witness compels truth, not memory."

"Unless someone has tampered with his memories," the Arbiter said quietly.

The courtroom went silent.

Elara's mind raced. Memory tampering was a high-level technique. Only cultivators at the peak of their power could manage it, and even then, it was risky. If done wrong, it could destroy the target's mind entirely.

"Who would do such a thing?" the head judge demanded.

No one answered.

But Elara noticed something. The hero's expression had shifted. Not to surprise or anger—to fear.

*He knows something.*

"Your Honor," Elara said, rising, "I request permission to question the witness."

"Denied. You are not a licensed—"

"I'm not questioning him about the poison." Elara's voice cut through the judge's objection. "I'm questioning him about his memory loss. If someone is powerful enough to tamper with a witness's mind in the presence of the Divine Witness, that person might be in this courtroom."

The judges exchanged glances.

"Permission granted. But be careful, accused. The Witness will hold you to your words."

Elara approached Zhang Wei. He flinched as she drew near, but she kept her movements slow, non-threatening.

"Mr. Zhang," she said gently, "I'm not going to hurt you. I just want to understand. When did you first realize your memories were missing?"

"A month ago. Maybe two." He shook his head. "Time is fuzzy. I woke up one morning, and I knew something was wrong. Like I'd forgotten to do something important."

"Did you seek help? A healer, a spiritual doctor?"

"No." He laughed bitterly. "Who would help a mortal? We're disposable. Everyone knows that."

*That's the problem,* Elara thought. *In this world, mortals are worthless.*

"Think carefully," she said. "Before the memory loss, did anything unusual happen? Anyone visit you who seemed out of place?"

Zhang Wei's brow furrowed. "I... there was someone. A man. He came to my shop, but he didn't want to buy anything. He just... looked at me. For a long time."

"Can you describe him?"

"I can't remember his face." Zhang Wei's voice cracked. "I can't remember anything about him except that he was tall. And he wore a cloak."

"A cloak? What color?"

"Black. No. Purple." He clutched his head. "I don't know! Every time I try to remember, it's like trying to hold smoke!"

The crystal pedestal pulsed again, and Elara felt a pressure against her mind. The Divine Witness was testing her, making sure she wasn't lying about her intentions.

*I'm not the one who needs to worry.*

"Thank you, Mr. Zhang." Elara turned to the judges. "Your Honors, I submit that the witness's testimony is compromised. The prosecution's evidence is tainted by a third party's interference. I move to dismiss the first charge."

"Overruled," the head judge said. "The witness's memory issues do not prove the accused's innocence. They merely cast doubt on the evidence."

*That's exactly what I wanted.*

"The prosecution may proceed," the judge continued.

The hero's jaw tightened. He'd lost momentum, and he knew it.

"Second charge," he said, his voice harder now. "The accused did conspire with the Shadow Flame Sect to assassinate the Celestial Sword Sect's Grand Elder."

This time, he didn't bother with witnesses. He produced a letter, sealed with the Demon Empress's personal crest.

"A written confession," the hero said. "Found in the Grand Elder's chambers after the assassination. It details the plan, the payment, and the method of execution."

*That's a forgery.*

Elara knew it instantly. The real Demon Empress had never written such a letter. She'd considered the Shadow Flame Sect beneath her notice.

But proving it was another matter.

"I request permission to examine the letter," Elara said.

The hero hesitated. "Your Honor—"

"Granted."

A servant brought the letter to Elara. She studied it carefully. The paper was old, the ink authentic. The handwriting matched the Demon Empress's—she'd seen enough of it in the palace archives to recognize the style.

But something was wrong.

The date.

The letter was dated three months ago. But the Demon Empress had been imprisoned for four months. She couldn't have written it while locked in a suppression cell.

"Your Honors," Elara said, "this letter is dated three months prior. But the Demon Empress was incarcerated four months ago. She could not have—"

"The letter could have been written before her imprisonment," the hero interrupted.

"Then why does it reference events that happened after?" Elara held up the letter. "It mentions the Grand Elder's death. But the Grand Elder was killed *six weeks* ago. The timeline doesn't match."

The hero's face went pale.

The judges examined the letter again. This time, they noticed what Elara had seen.

"The accused is correct," one judge said slowly. "The letter claims to detail a conspiracy that resulted in a death that had not yet occurred."

"That's impossible," the hero said.

"Is it?" Elara turned to face him. "Or is your evidence fabricated?"

*I have him.*

But the hero recovered quickly. "The letter is a prediction. The Shadow Flame Sect planned the assassination in advance. They wrote the letter before the Grand Elder died, expecting to claim credit afterward."

"That's not how assassinations work," Elara said. "You don't write confession letters before the crime is committed. That would be—"

"Madness?" the hero finished. "Exactly. We're dealing with demons, not rational beings."

The courtroom stirred. Elara could feel the tide turning against her.

"Your Honors," she said, trying a different approach, "I request the Divine Witness be applied to the letter itself."

"A document cannot be compelled to speak truth," the head judge said.

"Not to speak. To reveal. The Witness can show us who touched the letter, who wrote it, who handled it. If the evidence is forged, the Witness will expose the forgery."

The hero's composure cracked. "That's unprecedented."

"So was summoning the Divine Witness to a routine trial," Elara shot back.

The judges conferred again. The crystal pedestal pulsed, and for a moment, Elara felt something cold brush against her mind.

*The Witness is curious about me.*

"Very well," the head judge said. "The request is granted. But know this—if the letter is genuine, the accused will face immediate judgment. There will be no further appeals."

Elara nodded. It was a risk. But she was running out of options.

The servant took the letter and placed it on the crystal pedestal. The liquid starlight swirled, reaching out tendrils that touched the paper.

Then the Witness screamed.

It wasn't a sound. It was a psychic shattering that sent everyone in the courtroom to their knees. Elara felt her mind split open, her secrets laid bare—

The vision hit her like a tidal wave.
